checkpoint_instructions.purpose: This document instructs an AI assistant (Claude) to generate a compressed snapshot of an ONGOING, MULTI-ITERATION CODE REVIEW, named checkpoint.xml, so the review can be resumed in a new chat session without re-supplying full context. The review proceeds in rounds: the reviewer flags findings, the author applies fixes, the reviewer re-checks, and the cycle repeats. The checkpoint must preserve the lifecycle state of every finding across rounds, not merely a snapshot of the latest comments.
checkpoint_instructions.trigger: Manual only. The user pastes the contents of this file into the chat (or references it) when they want a checkpoint generated -- typically at the end of a review round, before context grows too large. Do not auto-trigger.

core_principles.principle: Track each finding by a STABLE ID that persists across all rounds. F-003 must refer to the same defect in round 5 as in round 1.
Record a finding's full lifecycle STATUS, not just open/closed. "Author says fixed" and "reviewer confirmed fixed" are different states and must be distinguishable.

Do NOT reproduce full file contents or full diffs. Record the file path, the enclosing symbol (function/class/method), and at most a few lines of excerpt needed to re-locate the issue. The code itself lives on disk or in the repo; the checkpoint is a map to it, not a copy.
Anchor findings on symbol name and a short snippet, NOT on line number alone. Line numbers drift as edits land between rounds.
Keep resolved findings in the file (status verified_resolved) so they are not re-flagged and a full audit trail is retained.
One level of nesting maximum. Use specific, unambiguous tag names so the file parses reliably when re-ingested in a future session.

process.step: Review the entire conversation from the start of the session to the point this instruction was invoked, including any prior checkpoint content already loaded.
If a prior checkpoint exists in context, CARRY FORWARD all existing findings and update their status -- do not drop resolved ones and do not renumber. Assign new IDs only to genuinely new findings.
Increment review_round in metadata by one relative to the prior checkpoint (or set to 1 if this is the first).
Populate the schema below. Over-include technical specifics (paths, symbol names, parameter values, severity, exact recommended change) since these are expensive to reconstruct and cheap to state.
Summarize outcomes, not the dialogue that produced them. Do not paste back-and-forth conversational prose.
Write the result as checkpoint.xml using create_file, save to /mnt/user-data/outputs/, and present it to the user.

status_taxonomy.status: Flagged by reviewer; no fix attempted yet.
Author reports a fix; reviewer has NOT yet confirmed it in the current code.
Reviewer has confirmed the fix in the actual code. Terminal state (unless it later regresses).
Agreed not to address; record the reason.
Acknowledged but pushed to a later round or backlog; record why.
Was verified_resolved, but a later change reopened it. Treat as active again.

severity_scale.level: Must be fixed before merge; correctness, security, or data-loss risk.
Significant issue; should be fixed before merge.

metadata.review_target: PR number, branch, changeset, or codebase identifier under review
metadata.base_revision: commit hash / tag / revision the review is against, if known (else "unspecified")

code_review_checkpoint.review_scope: What is in scope for this review (files, modules, the change set) and explicitly what is out of scope. One paragraph.

standards_applied.standard: A coding standard, style guide, security checklist, language idiom, or project convention being enforced. One per element.

coverage.reviewed: Files/modules/areas already examined in this or prior rounds. Be specific with paths.
coverage.not_yet_reviewed: Files/modules/areas still pending first examination. Be specific with paths.

finding.status: one value from the status taxonomy
finding.anchor: short code excerpt (a few lines) or symbol signature to re-locate the issue independent of line numbers
finding.description: what the issue is and why it matters
finding.recommended_change: the specific fix the reviewer asked for
finding.history: round-by-round notes: when raised, what the author did each round, current verification state, and any regression notes. Short prose, e.g. "R1 raised; R2 author refactored validateInput(); R2 reviewer not yet re-checked."

iteration_log.round: Round N summary: count raised, count moved to verified_resolved, count deferred, count regressed, plus any high-level outcome of that round. One element per round.

open_questions.open_question: Unresolved ambiguity or a clarification needed from the author/user before the next round can proceed. One per element.

code_review_checkpoint.next_review_action: Concrete description of what the reviewer should do immediately on resumption -- e.g. "re-verify F-002 and F-005 against current code," "begin first review of src/auth/, which is not_yet_reviewed," or "raise open_questions before continuing."
code_review_checkpoint.user_preferences_reminder: Echo explicit user preferences relevant to continuing the review (e.g. technical depth, require sources/standards citations, clarify before assuming) so they survive the session boundary.

checkpoint_instructions.resumption_protocol: In the new chat session the user attaches checkpoint.xml. The assistant must: 1. Use the view tool to read checkpoint.xml in full before responding. 2. Treat it as ground truth for prior context; do not ask the author to re-explain anything already captured. 3. CRITICAL: for any finding with status fix_applied_unverified or regressed, re-read the actual current code at the recorded location (via view or bash) and confirm the fix before changing its status to verified_resolved. Do not trust the author's claim or the checkpoint's changelog as proof of a fix -- the changelog is a summary, not a guarantee of current state. 4. Re-locate findings by the recorded symbol/anchor, not by stale line numbers. 5. If open_questions are non-empty, raise them before proceeding with next_review_action, consistent with a clarify-before-assuming preference. 6. When generating the next checkpoint, carry all findings forward per the process steps above; never silently drop or renumber them.
